I genuinely dislike how Nike brands the non-ZoomX foams as ZoomX in the shoes that include blends with other EVA’s. They’ve done it on quite a few in the lineup before, but the Vomero is a perfect example. It’s definitely a great shoe, however having that pinky-thin layer of ZoomX underfoot and then branding the midsole with ZoomX on the surface of its EVA carrier foam leads many to believe that it’s a ZoomX chunk there when that’s not the case. They do label them appropriately on the rear of the midsole, but it almost feels like it’s akin to fine print at the bottom of a contract. Not something I think is right, but I’m not a marketing expert and I guess it works for them even if it feels a bit scummy and disingenuous.

The Nike Pegasus Premium is packed with three different types of cushioning: Reactex at the bottom for stability, the first-ever sculpted full-length Zoom Air unit, and ultra-soft ZoomX foam at the top (0:42-0:56).
The Jordan 1 85 Bread had an extremely limited release, with only about 10,000 pairs available originally (1:39-1:42).
Jordan Brand announced an identical Jordan 1 85 Bread pair will be released again before the end of the year, differing only by an extra set of laces (1:52-2:07). This could potentially devalue the original release (2:17-2:25).
The Jordan 1 Nigel Sylvester Better with Time is surprisingly more comfortable than a standard pair of Jordan 1s (3:46-3:54).
The Jordan 5 Black Metallic Reimagined is considered by the speaker to be one of the best reimaginings Jordan Brand has ever done since starting the series in 2020 (5:02-5:09).
The popularity of the Adidas Evo SL largely stems from word-of-mouth rather than collaborations or crazy marketing (6:32-6:37).
The Jordan 4 Undefeated was the first-ever Jordan collaboration, making it a significant piece of sneaker history that paved the way for future collaborations (11:11-11:26).
The Jordan 4 Nigel Sylvester Brick By Brick is ironically named, as "brick" in sneaker resell terms means a shoe that doesn't sell well, but these are "the farthest things from bricks" (12:19-12:25).
The word "best" is said 7 times in the video (0:14, 0:25, 5:02, 6:01, 7:44, 11:00, 11:47).

Own two pairs - I know this guy runs on shoes for a living but I need to address dislikes for anyone that's curious about running or just running in these. I've got 270 miles on one shoe, pre-hype, apparently. Got a second pair with 30 miles because I'm a sucker for silver. He's reaching so, so deep.

1. Tongue / gusset are 100% fine. Have you ever had to adjust a tongue when putting a shoe on? Nobody cares at all unless you need youtube talking points. The tongues are very comfortable and don't move. They're shorter such that they don't fold and dig into your ankle. Much appreciated.
2. Laces - only the youtubers say they are cheap and aren't OK. Once you start using them you get it. They are 100% fine and actually do not feel cheap the more you use them. Are there fancier laces? Ohhhhk Yes, stretchy, ribbed. Glad I'm in a relationship because if this was my conversation starter, I'm cooked.
3. This is one of the best running shoes. Breathable (SoCal weather). Comfortable, relatively quiet, and wears like it was new after 270 miles. Seriously. Maybe a touch softer than the newer pair. Hard to tell.
4. Absolutely zero "lock down" issues even tho the toe box lets me stretch me toes wiiiide when I land. Could wear these barefoot. About the only thing he nailed is the instability - if you think you are changing directions quickly in these you might leave your foot behind you unless you have NHL ankles. Go straight.

Everyone has different feet for fit issues. But the concept and quality of these shoes is A+ based on me running wayyyyyy more miles in them. I'm trying to be faster than the strength trainers, and stronger than the runners. Gray zone. If your daily runs are easy 3-5 miles, a track sprint day, and occasional 10 milers like me... you'll love them.

Everyone already knows they stretch a lot, the lockdown starts sloppy and get worse, and they need to be actively cleaned and maintained with leather conditioner. And that they weigh 300ish grams dry. Here are the other cons I see noted much less often:

- They easily exceed 400 grams in wet conditions. This is not trivial. With two boots, that's approaching an extra kilogram of weight on your feet. We were used to that in the 20th century, but modern boots not absorbing water is a very good thing about modern boots.

- The soleplate is thin enough that you will feel stud pressure on 2G/3G artificial grass, or any AG pitch that needs renovation.

- Related to the previous, the soleplate and studs are not up to modern density standards -- they're soft. This causes the studs to wear down very quickly on any kind of AG (even high quality AG).

Despite having concials, these boots designed in 1979 were engineered for use on natural grass, and only natural grass. It's important people understand this. I see these boots recommended for AG so often, just because they have conicals, and it's bad advice.

for those who don’t know, the Five Nights at Freddy’s (FNaF) story is a long, tangled timeline centered around haunted animatronics, missing children, and the consequences of dangerous experiments tied to a company called Fazbear Entertainment, which started as a family-friendly restaurant chain featuring robotic mascots like Freddy Fazbear, Bonnie, Chica, and Foxy, but behind the scenes one of the founders, William Afton, secretly used the restaurants to lure and murder children, leading to their spirits possessing the animatronics and causing the strange and aggressive behavior seen throughout the games; Afton, often represented as the purple figure known as the “Purple Guy,” worked alongside his business partner Henry Emily, whose own daughter was one of Afton’s victims and later possessed the Puppet, an animatronic that tried to protect and give life to the other haunted robots, while Afton also involved his own family in tragic events, including his youngest son being accidentally killed after being placed too close to the animatronic Fredbear’s mouth during a birthday party, an event known as the Bite of ’83, and his daughter Elizabeth being killed by another animatronic Afton designed called Circus Baby, which was secretly built to capture children as part of Afton’s twisted experiments with a supernatural substance known by fans as remnant, believed to bind souls to metal; over time, Afton himself is spring-locked inside one of his own springlock suits, becoming the undead figure Springtrap, who repeatedly returns even after fires meant to destroy him, while various night guards—sometimes implied to be Afton’s surviving son Michael trying to fix his father’s mistakes—work at different Fazbear locations and experience the hauntings firsthand; the company repeatedly attempts to rebrand and reopen with new animatronics and technology, but each attempt is followed by more supernatural incidents, cover-ups, and disasters, eventually leading to efforts by Henry to gather all haunted animatronics into one final location to burn them and release the trapped souls, seemingly ending Afton and the haunted machines, though later entries in the series suggest Fazbear Entertainment continues using digital recreations and artificial intelligence that allow Afton’s influence to return in new forms, expanding the story into themes of corrupted technology, corporate denial, and the lingering consequences of the original crimes, which is why the timeline is famously complicated, filled with hidden clues, minigames, books, and multiple interpretations that fans are still debating.
